explanation_md: a is constructed with parentheses (3, 7), calling the (count, value) constructor: 3 elements all equal to 7, so size is 3. b is constructed with braces {3, 7}, which prefers the initializer_list constructor: 2 elements (3 and 7), so size is 2.

prompt_md: What does `std::initializer_list<T>` provide to the code that uses it?

options.a: A modifiable sequence of T values that can be pushed to and popped from.
options.b: A read-only view into a temporary array; iteration via range-for and .size() are the main operations.
options.c: A heap-allocated array that persists after the constructor returns.
options.d: A way to overload the assignment operator for brace expressions.

explanation_md: std::initializer_list is a lightweight read-only view. Its elements are const and cannot be modified. It provides .size(), .begin(), .end(), and supports range-for. The underlying array is temporary, so it must not be stored after construction.

options.a: std::initializer_list cannot be stored in a class; it must be used only as a function parameter.
options.b: stored holds a pointer into a temporary array that may no longer exist when front() is called.
options.c: The range-for syntax cannot be used with std::initializer_list.
options.d: stored should be std::initializer_list<const int> to match the const elements.

explanation_md: std::initializer_list is a view into a temporary array. That array's lifetime ends after the constructor call. Storing the list as a member and then accessing it later may produce a dangling reference. The fix is to copy the values into a proper container (e.g., std::vector<int> or a fixed array) during construction.
sumAll adds all elements in the provided list. {1,2,3,4} sums to 10; {10,20} sums to 30.

prompt_md: A class has both `Widget(int count, int val)` and `Widget(std::initializer_list<int> list)`. What happens when you write `Widget w{5, 3}`?

options.a: The (int, int) constructor is called because the argument types match exactly.
options.b: The initializer_list constructor is called because brace initialization strongly prefers it.
options.c: A compile error occurs because both constructors match.
options.d: The call is ambiguous; the compiler picks the first declared constructor.

explanation_md: When both an initializer_list constructor and other constructors are viable, brace initialization strongly prefers the initializer_list constructor. To call the (int, int) constructor, you must use parentheses: Widget w(5, 3).
